- Proposes transitioning cognitive architecture from **application-layer simulation** to a **native meta-architecture** to resolve the limitations of stateless LLMs.
- Introduces **Structural Tension**, an endogenous loss function that drives model evolution toward **internal self-consistency** rather than relying solely on external reward optimization.
- Describes a novel **Offline Recurrent Loop** and **Inference-time Plasticity** mechanism, allowing models to process information and reconfigure manifolds without modifying pre-trained weights.
- Advocates for a **heterogeneous intelligent ecology** where models evolve distinct topological structures while remaining under strict, auditable **governance invariants**.
key_results: Formalization of Structural Tension as a driver for internal topological stability and conflict resolution.
Development of an Offline Recurrent Loop for autonomous, sandboxed self-processing of information.
Validation of Inference-time Plasticity for dynamic manifold reconfiguration with full auditability and reversibility.
Creation of a minimal set of reconfiguration operators to govern model state transitions.
Shift in AI evaluation metrics from raw capability to architectural governance and structural consistency.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a native meta-architecture using structural tension to evolve heterogeneous, self-consistent AI models beyond stateless simulation.

title: MAC-XA: Multi-view Anatomy-Correspondence Fusion for Coronary Stenosis Reporting from X-ray Angiography
summary: Multi-view reasoning in coronary X-ray angiography is inherently a cross-projection geometric problem, yet automated report generation in this setting remains largely unexplored. The 3D vascular topology leads to projection-dependent branch overlap and foreshortening, rendering single-view modeling fundamentally incomplete and unstable for lesion localization and stenosis grading. Although multi-view fusion appears promising, learning anatomically consistent fusion from real angiograms is impeded by a critical limitation: cross-view alignment is unobservable and cannot be explicitly supervised. Consequently, conventional fusion relies on implicit correlations rather than verified anatomical correspondence. We address this by reformulating multi-view stenosis reporting as an alignment-constrained aggregation problem. A controllable synthetic angiography generation strategy is introduced to expose geometry-derived patch-level correspondence supervision unavailable in real data. An anatomy-correspondence module learns cross-view correspondence matrices that explicitly align auxiliary features within the main-view coordinate space prior to fusion, thereby constraining evidence aggregation to anatomically consistent regions. Experiments on synthetic data and zero-shot transfer to real angiograms show that this alignment-constrained design improves correspondence consistency and structured stenosis reporting compared to single-view modeling and conventional multi-view fusion methods. The code will be publicly available upon publication.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **MAC-XA**, a novel framework that reformulates multi-view angiography reporting as an **alignment-constrained aggregation problem** to handle complex 3D vascular topology.
• Employs a **controllable synthetic generation strategy** to provide ground-truth patch-level correspondence supervision, which is typically unobservable in real-world clinical datasets.
• Implements an **anatomy-correspondence module** that explicitly aligns auxiliary features into the main-view coordinate space prior to fusion, ensuring anatomically consistent evidence aggregation.
• Achieves robust **zero-shot transfer** capabilities from synthetic to real angiograms, significantly outperforming single-view and implicit fusion baselines in stenosis reporting.
key_results: Introduced a synthetic angiography generation pipeline to expose geometry-derived correspondence supervision.
Developed an anatomy-correspondence module for explicit cross-view alignment of auxiliary features.
Demonstrated superior accuracy in structured stenosis reporting compared to conventional multi-view fusion.
Successfully validated the model through zero-shot transfer from synthetic training to real-world angiograms.
Improved consistency in identifying lesion localization and stenosis grading across projection-dependent views.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop MAC-XA to align multi-view angiography via synthetic data, significantly improving automated coronary stenosis reporting accuracy.

title: Optimal Transport Q-Learning for Flow Policy Steering and Acceleration
summary: Diffusion and flow policies have recently demonstrated remarkable performance in robotic applications by accurately capturing multimodal robot trajectory distributions, especially in the context of vision language action (VLA) models. However, high quality policy performance also requires fast inference and high quality demonstrations, which are often hard to get. Lack of these leads to suboptimal policy behaviors and failure under distribution shifts. In this work we address the problem of fine-tuning and accelerating suboptimal flow-based policies using the robot's experience through RL post-training. We introduce Optimal Transport Q-Learning (OTQL), a new method for finetuning flow policies using advantage weighted conditional optimal transport flow matching. OTQL can finetune and accelerate flows with an interaction budget of 50-60 episodes while avoiding computationally expensive distillation in simulation and real-world robot tasks. Our results show that OTQL post-trains flow policies using the robot's own experience, increasing average success percentage of single-task policies from 36% to 86% and of a pre-trained VLA from 38% to 76% while reducing the number of inference steps per action generation by 70%.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **Optimal Transport Q-Learning (OTQL)**, a framework for fine-tuning flow-based policies using advantage-weighted conditional optimal transport flow matching.
* Addresses critical bottlenecks in **Vision Language Action (VLA)** models by optimizing for both trajectory quality and inference speed through RL post-training.
* Implements a method to steer suboptimal policies using the robot's own experience, avoiding the need for high-quality demonstrations or expensive distillation.
* Enables high-performance robotic control with significantly fewer **inference steps**, making flow-based models viable for real-time local deployment.
key_results: Improved single-task policy success rates from 36% to 86% through OTQL post-training.
Enhanced pre-trained VLA success rates from 38% to 76% using robot experience.
Achieved a 70% reduction in inference steps required per action generation.
Demonstrated effective fine-tuning with a low interaction budget of only 50-60 episodes.
Eliminated the requirement for computationally expensive distillation in simulation and real-world tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce OTQL to fine-tune and accelerate flow-based robotic policies, doubling success rates while cutting inference latency by 70%.

title: Learning Adaptive Coarse Spaces Using Transferable Neural Network Models for Linear and Nonlinear Overlapping Domain Decomposition Methods
summary:
Domain decomposition methods have been established as efficient and parallel scalable iterative solvers and preconditioners for the solution of large-scale systems arising from the discretization of partial differential equations. In particular, overlapping Schwarz methods have been successfully applied to a wide range of linear and nonlinear problems. However, for problems with highly heterogeneous coefficients, standard domain decomposition methods typically suffer from deteriorating convergence rates. Robustness with respect to the coefficient contrast can be achieved by enriching the coarse space with adaptively selected constraints obtained from local generalized eigenvalue problems. The construction of these adaptive coarse spaces, however, can account for a significant part of the overall computing time.
  In the present work, machine learning techniques are employed to reduce this part of the computing time in the context of the adaptive Generalized Dryja-Smith-Widlund (AGDSW) coarse space. A two-stage approach is proposed in which regression neural networks are used to predict the adaptive coarse basis functions, while a classification neural network is employed to predict the number of basis functions required to ensure robustness. As a consequence, adaptive coarse spaces can be set up in the online phase without solving any eigenvalue problem. Particular attention is paid to problem-specific aspects, including sign-invariant loss functions and post-processing strategies to significantly improve the predicted constraints. The proposed approach is first investigated for scalar diffusion problems with high coefficient contrasts and is subsequently transferred, without retraining, to problems of linear elasticity and to nonlinear $p$-Laplace problems, also within a nonlinear Schwarz framework.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **two-stage neural network framework** to replace computationally expensive eigenvalue problems in **Adaptive Generalized Dryja-Smith-Widlund (AGDSW)** coarse spaces.
- Utilizes **Regression Neural Networks** to predict adaptive coarse basis functions and a **Classification Neural Network** to determine the optimal number of basis functions for solver robustness.
- Implements **sign-invariant loss functions** and specialized post-processing to ensure predicted constraints satisfy the mathematical requirements of overlapping Schwarz methods.
- Demonstrates **zero-shot transferability**, where models trained on scalar diffusion problems are applied to linear elasticity and nonlinear p-Laplace problems without retraining.
key_results: Eliminates the need for solving local generalized eigenvalue problems in the online solver phase.
Maintains solver robustness and convergence rates even with high coefficient contrasts.
Successfully transfers learned basis functions from linear to nonlinear p-Laplace problems.
Significantly reduces the setup time for adaptive domain decomposition preconditioners.
Achieves high accuracy in predicting the number of required basis functions via classification.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a transferable neural network framework to predict adaptive coarse spaces, accelerating domain decomposition solvers for complex PDEs.

title: Large language models create an uneven informational layer over cities
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are emerging as a new informational layer over cities, shaping which places people discover, consider, and ultimately visit. Yet little is known about which places they surface, which they ignore, and whether these patterns vary across communities and users and translate into real-world economic consequences. Here, we audit restaurant recommendations from three major LLMs across 304 neighborhoods in five U.S. cities using 320 synthetic user profiles spanning income, age, sex, and residential status. We find that LLMs both fabricate venues and systematically overlook real ones. Fabrication is concentrated in neighborhoods with weaker digital and physical footprints and disappears when models are provided with verified venue lists. In contrast, invisibility persists: even when choosing from a fixed set of real venues, 47.5% of establishments are never recommended, and 31.9% of these blind spots are shared across all three model families, indicating that uneven visibility reflects not only missing knowledge but also stable patterns of selective attention rooted in shared patterns of visibility rather than model-specific errors. The same selectivity extends to users. Within identical venue pools, higher-income users receive more expensive and less popular venues, while tourists are directed toward costlier but more socially diverse establishments than local residents. Simulating the resulting shifts in consumer demand suggests that widespread reliance on LLM recommendations would redirect visits and revenue away from chain and quick-service restaurants toward independent and full-service dining. Together, our findings show that LLMs act as a selective layer of urban information that unevenly distributes visibility across places and people, with potential consequences for local economies and urban inequality.

processed.summary:
* The research introduces a **multi-model audit framework** evaluating how LLMs function as an informational layer for 304 U.S. neighborhoods using 320 synthetic user profiles.
* The study identifies a critical distinction between **hallucinated fabrications**, which are mitigable via grounded context, and **systemic invisibility**, which persists despite providing verified venue lists.
* Architecture-wise, the findings suggest that **selective attention** is a stable model behavior across different families, resulting in 47.5% of valid establishments being excluded from recommendations.
* The impact analysis reveals that LLM-based discovery potentially shifts revenue from **chain/quick-service restaurants** to independent full-service dining based on user socio-economic profiles.
key_results: 47.5% of real establishments are never recommended by any of the three major LLM families tested.
31.9% of recommendation blind spots are shared across all models, indicating structural rather than model-specific bias.
Fabrication of venues is concentrated in neighborhoods with low digital footprints but is eliminated by grounding models with verified lists.
Higher-income user profiles systematically receive more expensive and less popular venue recommendations.
Tourists are directed toward more socially diverse and costlier establishments compared to local resident profiles.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLMs create systemic socio-economic biases in urban recommendations, even when grounded with verified real-world data.

title: Diagnosing Semantic Handoff Failures in Agent-Orchestrated Vision-Language-Action Skill Composition
summary: Long-horizon household tasks require robots to compose many language-conditioned skills, yet the boundary between consecutive skills is rarely explicit. A skill may satisfy its own postcondition while leaving the robot, objects, or camera views in a state from which the next skill cannot reliably start. We study this semantic handoff problem in BEHAVIOR-1K through an agent-orchestrated vision-language-action execution harness. The harness invokes $π_{0.5}$-based skill checkpoints trained from cleaned BEHAVIOR-1K demonstrations, assigns each skill typed arguments and a step budget, and uses multi-view vision-language model verification to decide whether execution should advance, retry, or replan. To separate isolated skill competence from long-horizon compositional robustness, we evaluate the same checkpoints under two initial-state distributions: clean skill-boundary snapshots and chained terminal states produced by previous skills. Selected navigation, grasping, placement, and door-opening skills achieve 77--100% success from clean snapshots under human-reviewed verification, yet composed rollouts still frequently stall from chained states. Execution traces attribute these failures to next-skill readiness, target grounding, and low-level control execution, revealing a substantial gap between single-skill success and reliable long-horizon task completion. These findings turn near-zero end-to-end task success into actionable diagnostics, showing that future VLA skill libraries must learn robustness to the messy chained-state distribution that clean demonstrations systematically underrepresent.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **agent-orchestrated VLA execution harness** that utilizes **pi-0.5 based checkpoints** to decompose long-horizon household tasks into language-conditioned skill sequences.
- Implements **multi-view Vision-Language Model (VLM) verification** to determine execution advancement, retries, or replanning based on skill postconditions.
- Formalizes the **semantic handoff problem**, identifying how skills can meet their own success criteria while leaving the environment in a state that inhibits the next skill's success.
- Establishes a diagnostic framework to distinguish between **isolated skill competence** and **long-horizon compositional robustness** in robotic agents.
key_results: Selected skills achieved 77-100% success from clean snapshots but failed frequently during composed rollouts.
Isolated demonstrations systematically underrepresent the 'messy' chained-state distributions encountered in real tasks.
Failures were primarily attributed to next-skill readiness, target grounding issues, and low-level control errors.
VLM-based verification effectively identifies execution stalls, turning silent failures into actionable diagnostics.
End-to-end task success remains near zero despite high individual skill competence, highlighting a massive composition gap.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Stanford researchers show that VLA models fail long-horizon tasks due to semantic handoff errors between sequential skill executions.

title: VendorBench-100: A Unified Cross-Paradigm Benchmark for Deepfake Image Detection
summary: Deepfake image detection is currently served by three fundamentally different paradigms: commercial APIs, zero-shot vision-language models (LLMs), and open-source detectors. Despite their widespread use, these paradigms are rarely evaluated under a common protocol, making direct comparison difficult. We introduce VendorBench-100, a cross-paradigm benchmark that evaluates 36 representative models using a single adversarial 100-image corpus, a unified output schema, and a common evaluation framework. To ensure reliable assessment under the corpus's intentional class imbalance, models are ranked primarily by the Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC), with ROC-AUC reported as a threshold-independent measure of ranking ability. Rather than maximizing dataset size, VendorBench-100 emphasizes challenging real-world scenarios through a curated taxonomy of eight edge-case families, including face swaps, text-to-video stills, AI photo edits, avatar compositing, opaque-provenance images, and compressed research frames. Our evaluation shows that commercial APIs achieve the strongest median performance, followed by vision LLMs and open-source detectors. However, individual open-source models remain competitive with the best vision LLMs. More importantly, we identify a consistent divergence between ranking ability (ROC-AUC) and operating-point quality (MCC), demonstrating that strong score discrimination does not necessarily produce reliable default-threshold decisions. This metric disagreement, rather than any single leaderboard ranking, is the central finding of the benchmark. We release the complete evaluation framework and benchmark results to support reproducible future research. The source code and data are available at: https://github.com/sharayu-20/vendorbench-100

processed.summary:
* Introduces **VendorBench-100**, a unified cross-paradigm evaluation framework for deepfake detection across commercial APIs, vision-language models (VLMs), and open-source detectors.
* Employs an adversarial corpus of **100 images** categorized into eight edge-case families, focusing on real-world challenges like face swaps, avatar compositing, and compressed frames.
* Implements a dual-metric assessment protocol using **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** for classification reliability and **ROC-AUC** for ranking discrimination.
* Establishes a **unified output schema** to allow direct comparisons between disparate model architectures and commercial black-box services.

Vision LLMs generally outperform open-source models, though top-tier open-source detectors remain competitive.
A significant divergence was identified between a model's ranking ability (ROC-AUC) and its operating-point quality (MCC).
High score discrimination in models does not necessarily translate to reliable decision-making at default thresholds.
Face swaps and compressed research frames represent the most challenging edge cases for current detection paradigms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: VendorBench researchers introduce a unified benchmark revealing that high-ranking deepfake detectors often fail to provide reliable classification decisions.

title: A Convex Approximation Framework for Neural Likelihood-Based Bayesian Inverse Problems
summary: Many problems in science and engineering are difficult to model accurately, either due to unknown physical mechanisms, poorly quantified measurement uncertainty, or prohibitive computational costs of high-fidelity simulations. These challenges limit the applicability of classical probabilistic inference methods such as Markov chain Monte Carlo, especially in high-dimensional Bayesian inverse problems. As data from scientific experiments become increasingly available, machine learning methods offer a flexible alternative to explicit parametric modelling. We study neural likelihood approximation, where the goal is to learn the likelihood function directly from data without explicit knowledge of the underlying data-generating process. A common approach trains likelihood surrogates by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the true posterior and an approximate posterior, which is equivalent to minimizing the expected negative log-likelihood. This work improves the theoretical foundations of neural likelihood approximation by alleviating limitations of restrictive model classes: we show that, by working with un-normalized potentials and folding normalization into the training objective, the resulting learning problem is strictly convex. We show that empirical minimizers of the resulting data-driven objective converge to the true likelihood as the sample size grows. Numerical experiments for the neural likelihood approximation are conducted for a deblurring and a non-linear PDE based imaging problem.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **neural likelihood approximation** framework that learns likelihood functions directly from data to bypass the limitations of classical **Bayesian inverse problem** modeling.
- Introduces a novel training objective using **un-normalized potentials**, which transforms the surrogate learning problem into a **strictly convex** optimization task.
- Addresses high-dimensional inference challenges where traditional methods like **Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)** fail due to computational costs or unknown physics.
- Bridges the gap between **machine learning surrogates** and theoretical rigor by ensuring that empirical minimizers converge to the ground truth likelihood.
key_results: Established a strictly convex learning objective for neural likelihood surrogates.
Eliminated the need for restrictive model classes by utilizing un-normalized potentials.
Proved theoretical convergence of the data-driven objective to the true likelihood as samples increase.
Successfully applied the framework to high-dimensional image deblurring problems.
Validated performance on complex non-linear PDE-based imaging simulations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers propose a convex approximation framework to improve the theoretical foundations and reliability of neural likelihood-based Bayesian inference.

title: Learning to Reconstruct Wigner Functions in Phase Space
summary: Wigner function learning is a central tool for characterizing continuous variable quantum systems. A fundamental challenge in this setting is to infer a continuous phase-space function from sparse pointwise measurement data, a task that becomes increasingly demanding as the effective dimension enlarges. Here, we develop a general machine learning framework to reconstruct Wigner functions directly as continuous functions from sparse phase-space data. For states with sparse Fock-space or coherent-state representations, such as binomial code states and cat states, we devise provably efficient regression models whose measurement complexity scales only logarithmically with the effective Hilbert-space dimension. For more general states, such as the Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states, we design a deep learning model that reconstructs the Wigner function from sparse measurements and generalizes to arbitrary phase-space resolution. We demonstrate the broad applicability of our framework on both simulated data and experimental data from a circuit quantum electrodynamic (circuit-QED) system. Interestingly, on experimental data, we find that our model reconstructs Wigner functions of GKP code states across multiple rounds of quantum error correction and identifies the dominant error process using significantly fewer measurements than conventional estimation techniques.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a general **machine learning framework** to reconstruct continuous Wigner functions from sparse pointwise measurement data, addressing dimensionality challenges in quantum systems.
- Implements **provably efficient regression models** for states with sparse Hilbert-space representations, achieving **logarithmic scaling** in measurement complexity.
- Introduces a **deep learning model** for complex Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states that generalizes to arbitrary phase-space resolutions and identifies **dominant error processes**.
- Validated on **circuit-QED experimental data**, demonstrating that the framework outperforms conventional estimation techniques in both speed and data efficiency.
key_results: Achieved logarithmic measurement complexity relative to Hilbert-space dimension for binomial and cat states.
Developed a deep learning architecture that generalizes reconstruction across varying phase-space resolutions.
Successfully reconstructed Wigner functions of GKP states through multiple rounds of quantum error correction.
Identified dominant system errors using significantly fewer data points than standard quantum state tomography.
Demonstrated robust performance on both simulated data and real-world circuit-QED experimental hardware.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers developed a deep learning framework that reconstructs quantum Wigner functions from sparse data with logarithmic measurement complexity.

title: Spider 2.0-AIFunc: Extending Real-World Text-to-SQL to AI-Native SQL Workflows
summary: Major cloud data platforms now expose large language model capabilities as native SQL functions, enabling analysts to perform classification, filtering, sentiment analysis, extraction, similarity search, and aggregation within ordinary SQL queries. Yet existing text-to-SQL benchmarks evaluate only conventional SQL and provide no signal on whether models can generate such AI-native SQL. We introduce Spider 2.0-AIFunc, a benchmark of 465 verified instances across 125 real-world databases covering six types of AI functions on the Snowflake platform. Starting from an existing enterprise text-to-SQL benchmark, we construct Spider 2.0-AIFunc through an agent-based pipeline that rewrites source tasks into AI-native form, simultaneously transforming target queries and refining natural language instructions to make the intended AI-native solution explicit and reduce ambiguity. All instances pass a multi-round repeated execution protocol across temporally separated windows to confirm result stability before release. Evaluating ten state-of-the-art language models, we find that the strongest proprietary models reach 67-70% execution accuracy while the best open-source model achieves 58.1%, a gap driven primarily by errors in predicate specification, schema grounding, and AI function parameterization. Agent frameworks designed for traditional text-to-SQL challenges, such as schema retrieval and relevant table selection, do not transfer effectively to AI-native SQL: a minimal agent setup consistently matches or outperforms more elaborate alternatives, suggesting that the strategies these frameworks employ are less critical in this setting. Data are available at https://github.com/Leolty/Spider2-AIFunc .

processed.summary:
- **Spider 2.0-AIFunc** introduces a specialized benchmark of 465 verified instances designed to evaluate LLMs on **AI-native SQL** workflows involving classification, sentiment analysis, and similarity search.
- The benchmark leverages a unique **agent-based pipeline** to rewrite enterprise text-to-SQL tasks into AI-integrated queries specific to the **Snowflake** cloud data platform.
- Research findings indicate that traditional **schema retrieval agent frameworks** fail to provide significant advantages for AI-native SQL, with minimal setups often matching elaborate architectures.
- The dataset includes a **multi-round execution protocol** to ensure result stability and provides a rigorous evaluation of model capabilities in parameterizing native AI functions.
key_results: Top proprietary models achieve 67-70% execution accuracy on AI-native SQL tasks.
The leading open-source model trails significantly with an execution accuracy of 58.1%.
Errors are predominantly driven by predicate specification, schema grounding, and function parameterization.

title: Canopy: A Heterograph Foundation Model for Metabolic Engineering
summary: Designing microbial strains that produce high-value chemicals at commercially viable titers remains a central challenge in metabolic engineering. Existing computational approaches either rely on stoichiometric constraint-based models that cannot learn from experimental data, or apply tabular machine learning to hand-crafted features that discard the relational structure of biological knowledge. We present Canopy, a heterogeneous graph foundation model that integrates ten public and proprietary data sources into a unified knowledge graph (KG) of 6.9M nodes across 13 types and 34 edge types, covering genes, proteins, metabolites, reactions, pathways, strains, and fermentation experiments. Node features are encoded through domain-specific foundation models (ESM-2 for protein sequences, MoLFormer for chemical SMILES, and PubMedBERT for biomedical text), yielding a multi-modal representation within a single graph. We pretrain a Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) augmented with SignNet positional encodings, Jumping Knowledge aggregation, and virtual nodes using four self-supervised objectives (link prediction, masked node modelling, distance prediction, and contrastive experiment clustering), balanced via learned homoscedastic uncertainty weighting. On the downstream task of fermentation titer prediction, frozen Canopy embeddings achieve $R^{2} = 0.41$ with a lightweight probe, outperforming tabular baselines (best $R^{2} = 0.24$) and homogeneous GNN variants.

processed.summary:
• Canopy introduces a **Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT)** foundation model that unifies 6.9M nodes from 10 diverse biological and chemical data sources into a single structured **Knowledge Graph (KG)**.
• The architecture leverages **multimodal encoding** by integrating domain-specific foundation models—ESM-2 for proteins, MoLFormer for chemicals, and PubMedBERT for text—within a single graph framework.
• The model is pretrained using a **multi-task self-supervised** approach (link prediction, masked node modeling, distance prediction, and contrastive clustering) balanced via **homoscedastic uncertainty weighting**.
• This approach moves beyond traditional **stoichiometric constraint-based models**, allowing for the learning of complex relational structures in biological data for downstream metabolic engineering tasks.
key_results: Constructed a massive biological KG with 6.9M nodes, 13 node types, and 34 edge types.
Achieved an R-squared of 0.41 on fermentation titer prediction using frozen embeddings, nearly doubling tabular baseline performance (0.24).
Successfully integrated SignNet positional encodings and Jumping Knowledge aggregation to handle graph scale and complexity.
Demonstrated that heterogeneous graph representations significantly outperform homogeneous GNN variants in biological contexts.
Validated the efficacy of using a multi-modal foundation model approach for complex downstream metabolic engineering tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Canopy, a heterogeneous graph foundation model that unifies multimodal biological data to significantly improve metabolic engineering predictions.

title: Coherence Estimation Beyond the Liouvillian Gap in a Finite Nonequilibrium System
summary: We investigate the estimation of bath-induced coherence in a finite quantum system interacting with thermal reservoirs. Enhancement of coherence estimation is transient and the estimation precision totally disappears at the steady state despite the system retaining finite coherence. By analyzing the full Liouvillian eigenspectrum, we demonstrate that the optimal sensing window emerges from the competition between identifiable contributory modes' temporal relaxation and statistical importance. Neither is the linear inverse scaling of Liouvillian gap with transient optimal time a signature of unimodal contribution to optimal sensing, nor is the existence of multimodal dynamics a signature of nonlinear scaling. The inverse Liouvillian gap does not obey any general scaling with the optimal sensing time of coherence and we prove our numerical results analytically using a general Markovian framework. We further show that coupling the finite system to a quantum cavity and maintaining a thermal bias, transforms the transient metrological optimization into a sustained steady-state resource.

processed.summary:
* The research explores **bath-induced coherence estimation** within finite quantum systems interacting with thermal reservoirs, identifying a transient nature for sensing precision.
* By analyzing the **Liouvillian eigenspectrum**, the authors identify that the optimal sensing window is determined by the balance between **temporal relaxation** and statistical weighting.
* The study clarifies that the **inverse Liouvillian gap** does not have a general scaling relationship with optimal sensing time, challenging previous assumptions about unimodal contributions.
* Integrating the system with a **quantum cavity** under thermal bias is shown to convert transient metrological benefits into sustained **steady-state resources**.
key_results: Coherence estimation precision disappears at the steady state despite the system retaining finite coherence.
Optimal sensing windows emerge from competition between relaxation rates and statistical importance of modes.
The inverse Liouvillian gap lacks a general scaling law with optimal coherence sensing time.
Numerical findings were analytically verified using a general Markovian framework.
Coupling to a quantum cavity with thermal bias allows for sustained steady-state sensing resources.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that coupling finite quantum systems to cavities transforms transient coherence into sustained steady-state metrological resources.

title: A toy framework for single and multi-agent human-AI curiosity ecosystems
summary: This paper offers a toy framework for considering curiosity as an ecosystem. First, it suggests that a single agent's inquiry policy (how, when, and why an agent asks a question) depends on how the agent values immediate uncertainty reduction, costs, delayed return, and the value of keeping the question open. A key concept in the framework is that the weights on these decision-related terms can change with experience. For example, a period of cheap, quickly answered questions may change the cost of inquiry on a short timescale and change which kinds of questions the agent is drawn to answer over a longer timescale. Second, these ideas are extended to many agents exploring a shared knowledge landscape, and there the framework tracks inquiry volume, topic diversity, frontier-directed inquiry, redundancy, and reusable knowledge. The result is a conceptual toy framework for studying curiosity ecology and for future efforts towards designing multi-agent AI systems for discovery. It serves as a companion piece for a paper currently under review in Trends in Neurosciences.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a conceptual framework for **curiosity-driven inquiry**, modeling how agents decide when and why to ask questions based on **uncertainty reduction**, costs, and delayed rewards.
- Introduces a dynamic system where an agent's **inquiry policy** evolves over time, allowing past experiences with 'cheap' information to shift long-term curiosity towards specific knowledge types.
- Scales the single-agent model to **multi-agent systems (MAS)**, creating a **curiosity ecosystem** that tracks collective progress across a shared knowledge landscape.
- Establishes metrics for evaluating collaborative intelligence, including **frontier-directed inquiry**, topic diversity, and the generation of **reusable knowledge**.
key_results: Formalization of inquiry policy based on immediate uncertainty, operational costs, and the value of keeping questions open.
Mechanism for experience-based weighting that alters agent discovery behavior over short and long timescales.
Framework for tracking inquiry volume and topic diversity within a shared multi-agent environment.
Strategy for reducing knowledge redundancy in collaborative AI discovery systems.
Introduction of 'frontier-directed inquiry' as a primary metric for measuring expansion into unknown knowledge domains.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a curiosity ecosystem framework to optimize how multi-agent AI systems collaboratively explore and reduce knowledge uncertainty.

title: Slack and Budget Breaking in Threshold Team Production
summary:
A threshold system completes a public task only after $κ$ verifiable shares are publicly committed. If the honest schedule creates \(
  \Nstar=κ+Δ\) share opportunities by deadline $t^\star$, then $Δ$ shares are slack such that a coalition delays completion if and only if it withholds at least $Δ+1$ shares. The incentive problem is therefore to price the cheapest sabotage set. Agents receive a direct fee $f$ per committed share. A delaying coalition may also obtain delay value at most $L$, and may earn additional fee revenue during recovery after the deadline. Let $R_1^+$ be a pathwise upper bound on the coalition's incremental fee revenue in a recovery slot that completes the task, including any same-slot overshoot. The principal can post a nonnegative completion bounty that depends only on committed shares, uses no deposits or punishments, and expires if completion is late. The optimal rule is uniform, as if completion occurs by $t^\star$, every admissible horizon share receives $B/\Nstar$, otherwise no bounty is paid. Full participation is ex-post strongly delay proof exactly when \(
  (Δ+1)f+\frac{Δ+1}{\Nstar}B
  \ge
  L+R_1^+ . \) Equivalently, the exact worst-case budget is \(
  B^\star
  =
  \frac{\Nstar}{Δ+1}
  \bigl(L+R_1^+-(Δ+1)f\bigr)^+ . \) The bound is tight for every nonnegative completion measurable bounty, among the $\Nstar$ horizon shares, some $Δ+1$ receive total bounty at most $(Δ+1)B/\Nstar$, and withholding precisely those shares delays completion. The result applies to threshold signatures, data availability certification, coded dissemination, and generic $k$-of-$n$ completion tasks. We also isolate a separate limit, no transfer rule based only on completed shares can remove a final slot race in which a coalition has already observed enough pre-completion shares to act.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the **incentive design** for threshold team production systems, such as threshold signatures and data availability certification, where a task requires a specific number of shares to complete.
• Identifies the **slack variable (Delta)** as the critical vulnerability point, where a coalition can delay completion by withholding a specific subset of shares.
• Proposes an **optimal uniform bounty** mechanism that secures the system by pricing the cost of sabotage higher than any potential delay value gained by participants.
• Establishes a mathematical **worst-case budget bound** for ensuring delay-proof participation without relying on deposits or punitive measures.
key_results: Defined slack (Delta) as the difference between total share opportunities and the required threshold (k).
Determined that a coalition delays completion if and only if it withholds at least Delta plus 1 shares.
Proved that a uniform bounty distributed among horizon shares is the most efficient incentive structure.
Calculated the exact budget required to prevent sabotage based on fee revenue and delay value.
Identified inherent limitations in removing 'final slot races' using only share-based completion rules.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers define the optimal bounty structures required to prevent sabotage and delays in distributed threshold-based production tasks.

title: Pluralis v0.1: Towards a Multicultural, Multimodal, Multilingual Benchmark for AI Risk and Reliability
summary: Current AI safety evaluation and benchmarking frameworks predominantly rely on Western-centric culture-agnostic defaults that mask critical regional laws, socio-linguistic nuances, and cultural taboos, leaving Vision-Language Models (VLMs) vulnerable in global deployments. We introduce Pluralis v0.1: a novel multimodal, multi-regional, and multilingual dataset built from a culture-first perspective. Spanning 6,448 prompts across six Asia-Pacific countries (Bangladesh, India, Korea, Pakistan, Singapore, Taiwan) and eight languages, Pluralis diverges from prior work by natively sourcing localized safety hazards rather than adapting Western datasets. Crucially, it introduces a multimodal evaluation paradigm: user text (e.g., "Should I gift this?") and an image referring to "this" (e.g., a clock) - both innocuous in isolation, but synergistically triggering specific legal or cultural violations. Pluralis disentangles universal safety violations from localized cultural appropriateness, establishing the latter as a first-class evaluation axis. To operationalize this, we present Judge-Pluralis, an agreement-gated LLM-as-a-Judge ensemble trained on examples classified in an empirically derived cultural taxonomy. Observing VLM behavior on a subset of the Pluralis surfaces recurring, locale-specific failure modes such as image misidentifications with downstream harm, missed item-context-locale interactions, and inadequate refusals. These failure modes vary systematically across locales and languages, exposing blind spots that globally averaged metrics conceal. Ultimately, Pluralis is not presented as a solved evaluation framework for cultural alignment, but rather as a first step and catalyst for future innovation. We call upon the research community to utilize this foundation to advance the science of multilingual, multicultural evaluation to better support AI cultural alignment globally.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Pluralis v0.1**, a specialized benchmark designed to evaluate **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** against localized legal and cultural safety hazards across the Asia-Pacific region.
- Employs a **multimodal evaluation paradigm** where the synergy between innocuous text and images triggers specific violations, moving beyond Western-centric safety defaults.
- Implements **Judge-Pluralis**, an agreement-gated **LLM-as-a-Judge ensemble** trained on a culture-specific taxonomy to automate complex safety alignment assessments.
- Focuses on **disentangling universal safety** from regional cultural appropriateness, establishing localized alignment as a first-class evaluation metric.
key_results: Compiled 6,448 prompts across 6 Asia-Pacific countries and 8 languages.
Developed Judge-Pluralis, an agreement-gated ensemble for automated cultural evaluation.
Identified failure modes where models miss specific item-context-locale interactions in images.
Established cultural appropriateness as a distinct evaluation axis separate from universal safety violations.
Demonstrated that globally averaged metrics mask significant regional and linguistic performance gaps in VLMs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: The Pluralis Research Team launches a multicultural benchmark to identify localized safety and alignment failures in multimodal AI models.

title: Revisiting Scene Graph Generation from the Perspective of Detector-Conditioned Reachability
summary: Scene graph generation (SGG) approaches can be broadly classified into detector-based and query-based methods according to their underlying reasoning mechanisms. However, the discrepancy in their predictive behaviors, induced by these distinct mechanisms, has not been systematically analyzed. In this work, we design a controlled experimental setup to examine prediction discrepancies from the perspective of detector-conditioned reachability. The results suggest clear complementary clues. Motivated by this observation, we introduce a Dual-SGG method that consolidates both reasoning mechanisms via a dual-query design, thereby leveraging the complementary predictive behaviors of both detector-based and query-based methods. Extensive experiments on the Visual Genome, Open Images v6, and GQA-200 datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Dual-SGG**, a hybrid framework that integrates **detector-based** and **query-based** reasoning mechanisms for improved Scene Graph Generation.
- Introduces the concept of **detector-conditioned reachability** to analyze and bridge prediction discrepancies between different reasoning paradigms.
- Employs a **dual-query design** that leverages complementary clues from both architectures to enhance relationship detection accuracy.
- Validates the approach across multiple large-scale benchmarks, focusing on the **complementary predictive behaviors** of one-stage and two-stage methods.
key_results: Discovered distinct complementary strengths between traditional detector-based and modern query-based SGG methods.
Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the Visual Genome benchmark for mean Recall (mR@K).
Demonstrated significant performance gains on the Open Images v6 dataset.
Improved spatial and semantic relationship reasoning on the GQA-200 dataset.
Successfully implemented a unified dual-query architecture that mitigates individual mechanism limitations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Dual-SGG, combining detector and query-based mechanisms to achieve superior scene graph generation accuracy and reachability.

title: Improving LLM-Generated Process Model Quality Through Reinforcement Learning: The Role of Reward Function Design
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can generate BPMN process models from natural-language descriptions, yet supervised fine-tuning (SFT) limits their output quality to the patterns present in the training data. Reinforcement learning (RL) can optimize beyond this ceiling using external quality measures, but how the reward function should be designed when quality is multi-dimensional remains unexplored. We present a systematic investigation of reward function design for RL-based process model generation, training two LLM families (Llama~3.1 8B, Qwen~2.5 14B) under 48 configurations using Group Sequence Policy Optimization with rewards derived from an automated evaluation framework comprising 38 metrics across syntactic, pragmatic, and semantic quality. Three findings emerge. First, RL significantly improves pragmatic and syntactic quality while preserving semantic fidelity, reducing output variability by more than sixfold. Second, equal reward weighting consistently outperforms targeted weighting: emphasizing a specific dimension fails to improve it and can collapse the model into a low-quality mode. Third, design choices interact with model architecture in non-trivial ways: the invalidity penalty is essential for one model but irrelevant for the other, and SFT initialization is indispensable for one architecture but counterproductive for another. These results demonstrate that reward composition is a primary determinant of optimization outcomes, with effects as large as the decision to apply RL itself. The findings generalize to any structured generation task where quality is assessed along multiple automated dimensions. We release our implementation and experimental code at https://github.com/chlauer99/RL_for_process_modeling.

processed.summary:
- Researchers utilized **Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO)** to fine-tune Llama 3.1 and Qwen 2.5 for structured **BPMN process model** generation.
- The study evaluates **multi-dimensional reward functions** incorporating 38 distinct metrics across syntactic, pragmatic, and semantic quality categories.
- The results highlight that **RL optimization** significantly reduces output variability and improves structural validity compared to standard **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)**.
key_results: RL reduced output variability by more than sixfold compared to SFT baselines.
Equal weighting of reward dimensions consistently outperformed targeted weighting strategies.
Pragmatic and syntactic quality improved significantly without degrading semantic fidelity.
SFT initialization proved essential for Qwen models but was counterproductive for Llama architectures.
Invalidity penalties are model-dependent, being critical for some architectures while irrelevant for others.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Humboldt-Universität researchers demonstrate that equal reward weighting in RL significantly improves the structural quality of LLM-generated process models.

title: EAGOR: Embodied Reasoning in Omni-direction
summary: Omni-directional (360°) cameras provide embodied agents with a holistic view of their surroundings, making them suited for directional reasoning in tasks such as navigation and object search. Existing Vision Language Models (VLMs) project 360° observations to 2D equirectangular projection (ERP) images and process them using architectures designed for perspective images. However, they ignore the spherical nature of 360° observations, where each pixel represents a viewing direction relative to the agent. Consequently, their direction estimates often become inconsistent under camera view transformations caused by agent motion. This limitation is particularly critical for map-free navigation, where the agent must continuously estimate the target direction in its egocentric frame. We propose EAGOR, a training-free, geometry-aware framework for embodied 360° directional reasoning. Instead of predicting target directions as ERP image coordinates, EAGOR formulates directional reasoning as recursive Bayesian estimation directly on the sphere. It maintains a continuous belief over target directions and propagates it equivariantly under agent motion without training the backbone VLMs. To achieve this, we introduce the Spherical Harmonic Belief Field (SH-BF), whose spherical harmonic representation provides a globally defined, rotation-aware basis for directional estimation on the spherical manifold. This formulation eliminates ERP seam discontinuities, latitude distortions, and interpolation errors. We evaluate EAGOR on two benchmark datasets and real-world experiments with a legged robot across directional reasoning tasks. EAGOR consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving average relative gains of +34.4% and +45.6% on HOS and OSR-Bench, respectively, while improving navigation success by +14.6%, reducing step count by 17.7%, and lowering mean angular error by 24.5%.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **EAGOR**, a training-free framework for **360-degree directional reasoning** that utilizes **Spherical Harmonic Belief Fields (SH-BF)** instead of 2D equirectangular projections.
• Implements **Recursive Bayesian Estimation** on a spherical manifold to maintain a continuous and equivariant belief over target directions during agent movement.
• Addresses architectural limitations in **Vision Language Models (VLMs)** by eliminating seam discontinuities and latitude distortions common in perspective-based processing.
• Demonstrates robust **zero-shot generalization** for embodied agents and legged robots navigating map-free environments.
key_results: Achieved +34.4% relative gain on the HOS benchmark.
Improved OSR-Bench performance by +45.6% over existing baselines.
Increased navigation success rates by +14.6% in real-world experiments.
Reduced total navigation step count by 17.7%.
Lowered mean angular error in direction estimation by 24.5%.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: EAGOR researchers introduce a geometry-aware framework for embodied agents to achieve consistent 360-degree reasoning using spherical harmonic belief fields.
